era_context: Alexander continues his consolidation of the Persian Empire's eastern territories. Spitamenes leads a major rebellion in Sogdiana. In Greece, Macedonian control remains secure under Antipater. The Hellenistic world is being reshaped by Alexander's conquests. In China, the Warring States period continues with Qin's power growing. In India, the Nanda Empire remains dominant.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Detailed records from contemporary Qin, Indian, and Persian sources

events.title: Spitamenes' Rebellion in Sogdiana
events.description: Spitamenes, a Sogdian nobleman, leads a major revolt against Macedonian rule. He initially defeats Alexander's forces in several engagements and allies with nomadic tribes from the steppes. The rebellion represents the most serious resistance Alexander faces in Central Asia. Alexander responds with a series of campaigns to suppress the revolt, which continues intermittently over several years. The conflict introduces Alexander's army to steppe warfare and demonstrates the challenges of controlling Central Asian territories.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VII–VIII
Diodorus Siculus, Bibliotheca Historica, Book XVII

events.certainty_note: Spitamenes' rebellion is well-attested in multiple sources, but the exact chronology is uncertain. Modern scholars debate whether the rebellion began in 329 or 328 BCE and lasted until 327 or 326 BCE. Arrian's account is the most detailed but contains internal inconsistencies.

events.title: Alexander's Marriage to Roxana
events.description: Alexander marries Roxana (Roxshana), daughter of the Bactrian nobleman Oxyartes. The marriage is politically motivated, designed to legitimize Macedonian rule and integrate the Bactrian aristocracy into Alexander's regime. Roxana becomes Alexander's principal wife and bears him a son (Alexander IV) after his death. The marriage represents Alexander's policy of cultural fusion and integration of conquered peoples into his empire.

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book IV
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VIII

events.certainty_note: The marriage is attested in multiple sources, but dating is uncertain. Some sources place it in 328 BCE, others in 327 BCE. The exact year is debated by modern scholars, with estimates ranging from 329–327 BCE.

events.title: Philotas Conspiracy and Execution
events.description: Philotas, son of Parmenion and commander of the Macedonian cavalry, is accused of conspiracy against Alexander. He is tried, convicted, and executed. His father Parmenion, one of Alexander's most senior generals, is also executed in absentia. The purge removes potential rivals and consolidates Alexander's personal authority. The event marks a turning point in Alexander's reign, as he becomes increasingly autocratic and suspicious of his generals.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (judge and executor)

sources.name: Arrian, Anabasis of Alexander, Book III
Curtius Rufus, History of Alexander the Great, Book VI–VII

events.certainty_note: The Philotas conspiracy is well-attested in multiple sources. However, modern scholars debate whether the conspiracy was real or fabricated by Alexander. Dating is generally placed in 330 BCE by most scholars, though some sources suggest 329 or 328 BCE. The event itself is historically certain, but its interpretation is contested.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Spitamenes' rebellion is highly uncertain. While it is often associated with 328 BCE, some scholars place its beginning in 329 BCE and its end in 327 or 326 BCE. The exact chronology cannot be established from surviving sources. The Philotas conspiracy is traditionally dated to 330 BCE, not 328 BCE, by most modern scholars including Bosworth. Its inclusion here reflects uncertainty in the historical record. Modern scholarship increasingly questions whether the conspiracy was genuine or a fabrication by Alexander to eliminate a rival. The marriage to Roxana is dated variously to 329, 328, or 327 BCE across different sources; no definitive dating is possible.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 328 BCE remains entirely dependent on later Greek and Roman sources with no contemporary documentation. Arrian and Curtius Rufus are the primary sources, both writing centuries after the events. The accounts reflect the perspectives of Alexander's companions and later Hellenistic historians, with potential biases toward justifying Alexander's actions. The chronology of events in Central Asia is notoriously difficult to establish, and modern scholars continue to debate the exact sequence and dating. Archaeological evidence from Central Asia for this period is limited. The record is heavily weighted toward military and political events; the experiences of conquered populations, economic impacts, and cultural developments are largely invisible. Persian and Central Asian perspectives are entirely absent from surviving sources.

graph_edges.note: Spitamenes' rebellion continues and intensifies in 327 BCE
Marriage to Roxana results in birth of Alexander IV
Purge of generals continues Alexander's consolidation of power
